sentences.base_language: I'm hungry. Let's go to the restaurant.
sentences.grammarNote: 'Tener hambre' = to be hungry (literally 'to have hunger'). Spanish uses tener for physical states: tener hambre, tener sed (thirsty), tener frío/calor (cold/hot). Also: 'al' is the mandatory contraction of 'a + el'.

sentences.base_language: The waiter takes us to a table.
sentences.target_language: El camarero nos lleva a una mesa.

sentences.grammarNote: 'Nos' is the indirect object pronoun for 'us'. It comes before the verb: 'Nos da el menú' = He gives us the menu. Compare: 'me da' (gives me), 'te da' (gives you), 'le da' (gives him/her).

sentences.base_language: I want chicken with rice, please.
sentences.target_language: Quiero pollo con arroz, por favor.
sentences.grammarNote: 'Quiero' is the yo form of querer (to want). Querer is an e→ie stem-changing verb: quiero, quieres, quiere, queremos, queréis, quieren. The stem change happens in all forms except nosotros/vosotros.

sentences.base_language: Also a salad and some bread.

sentences.grammarNote: 'Para + infinitive' means 'in order to'. 'Para beber' = to drink / in order to drink. Compare with 'por', which is used for cause or exchange. 'Para' looks forward (purpose); 'por' looks back (reason).

sentences.base_language: The food arrives on a big plate. Delicious!
sentences.target_language: La comida llega en un plato grande. ¡Delicioso!

sentences.grammarNote: 'Después de + infinitive' = after doing something. The infinitive is always used after prepositions in Spanish: 'antes de comer' (before eating), 'sin pagar' (without paying), 'al llegar' (upon arriving).
